content.problemStatement:
Once again, the smart friends Johnny and Mary have invented a new game. This time, the game is played with their coins and a strip of paper divided into cells. The cells are numbered from the left, starting from 1.

Before the game starts, Johnny and Mary put some coins at some random cells on the strip, each coin in a cell. They alternatively take their turn to play the game. At each step, a player must take a coin and move it to a cell to the left of it. There is at most one coin in a cell at any time of the game. Of course, a coin cannot jump over another coin.

The rule is simple: whoever cannot move loses the game. Mary takes her turn first.

Could you tell them who will win the game, provided that the winner will play the perfect strategy? If Mary could win the game, show her a winning move. If there are several winning moves, show her the winning move that uses the leftmost possible coin. If there are still several moves, show her the move that moves the coin as far as possible to the left.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (the number of coins). The second line contains N distinct integers p1 p2 ... pN in strictly increasing order, representing the positions of the coins. Test cases may be separated by blank lines.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output either "Mary wins" or "Johnny wins" on a single line. If Mary can force a win, output a second line "Move a to b" where a is the original position of the chosen coin and b is its new position according to the optimal winning move. Print a blank line after each test case.

sampleTestCases.output:
Johnny wins

Mary wins
Move 5 to 4

Mary wins
Move 2 to 1
sampleTestCases.explanation: Case 1: gaps are [1,1]; XOR=0 so Mary loses. Case 2: gaps [0,1,2]; XOR=3. Leftmost coin with (gap XOR 3) < gap is the third coin (gap=2). New gap = 2^3=1, new position = 1+3=4. Case 3: gaps [1,2,3,5]; XOR=1. Leftmost suitable coin is the first (gap=1). New gap = 1^1=0, new position = 0+1=1.

solution.approach: Let the coins be at positions p1 < p2 < … < pN. The i‑th coin can be moved to any empty cell strictly greater than p_{i‑1} (or to 1 if i=1) and smaller than its current position. Define gap_i = p_i - i. The minimal reachable configuration is (1,2,…,N) whose gaps are all zero. It can be shown that each gap_i behaves like an independent Nim heap of size gap_i, because moving coin i reduces gap_i to any smaller non‑negative value without affecting gaps of other coins. Therefore the Grundy value of the whole position is XOR of all gap_i. If the XOR (nim_sum) is zero the current player (Mary) loses, otherwise she wins. To obtain a winning move, find the leftmost i such that (gap_i XOR nim_sum) < gap_i. Set new_gap = gap_i XOR nim_sum and move coin i to position new_gap + i. This move makes the overall XOR zero and, by the tie‑breaking rules, uses the leftmost possible coin and moves it as far left as possible.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case – a single pass to compute gaps and the XOR, plus another pass to locate the winning coin.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra besides the input array.
